behaviors.description: Speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so transcripts can attribute statements to specific attendees
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the code fails to correctly parse and preserve whatever format the speech-to-text API actually returns. Mocking the API with a single hardcoded format (e.g., `[Speaker_1]: text`) would pass, but the real API might return `Speaker: ...` (no brackets), JSON structure, or segment-wise labels with overlapping speakers. The parsing logic and data flow must handle the realistic API format—you can't mock this away. This requires Integration level: real (or very realistic) API outputs wired into the code, not a pure mock object.
behaviors.test_description: Create Integration test with realistic speech-to-text outputs (based on actual API documentation/examples). Parse output, verify speaker labels are extracted and preserved. Test edge cases: overlapping speakers, silent attendees, low-confidence segments. Assert downstream code receives correct speaker attributions.

behaviors.description: Structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee assigned a task during the meeting
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the LLM omits or misses action items in its output—either because it failed to extract them from the transcript, or because it hallucinated some but not others. Mocking the LLM with perfect data (all attendees have action items) would pass, hiding the core failure: the real LLM sometimes doesn't produce action items for all attendees. You could test code parsing logic at Integration level (mock LLM with realistic outputs), but you'd miss the LLM's failure mode. The invariant depends on what the LLM actually produces.
behaviors.test_description: Real LLM call with realistic meeting transcript (or representative examples from prior runs) that includes multiple attendees, some assigned tasks. Verify: (1) LLM output includes action items; (2) code correctly parses and attributes them; (3) at least one action item appears per assigned attendee. Test both clear cases and ambiguous cases ("Sarah or Mike should handle this"). Assert the invariant holds end-to-end.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Could Integration-level tests with realistic LLM fixtures catch parsing bugs. But the deeper failure—LLM omission—only surfaces with Agentic testing. Recommend both: cheap Integration tests with fixtures for regression, Agentic tests for LLM behavior verification.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in the narrative
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the LLM doesn't structure decisions into a separate section—it buries them in the narrative, or outputs decisions that aren't actually decisions. Mocking the LLM with properly structured output (a 'decisions' array containing only true decisions) would pass, hiding the real issue: the real LLM produces summaries where decisions are scattered throughout the narrative or missing. The code might parse the output correctly, but the LLM didn't do its job structuring it. Need a real LLM call to verify the summary structure matches the intended schema.
behaviors.test_description: Real LLM call with meeting transcript containing clear decisions (e.g., 'We decided to use AWS instead of GCP', 'The budget cap is $500k'). Verify: (1) LLM output has a 'decisions' section; (2) decisions appear there, not in narrative; (3) decisions are correct. Test edge cases: decisions mentioned early vs. late, implicit vs. explicit decisions. Assert structure matches schema.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration tests with fixtures would verify the code correctly parses a well-formed 'decisions' section. But whether the LLM actually produces one is Agentic. Both levels needed for full coverage.

behaviors.description: Audio files longer than 60 minutes are split into segments, processed sequentially, and results merged into a single coherent summary
behaviors.justification: The real failure is in the splitting/merging logic: the code doesn't detect the 60-min boundary correctly, splits at wrong points, processes out of order, or merges results incorrectly (duplicate content, missing content, broken speaker continuity across segments). You can test this with synthetic or realistic audio files and realistic transcription outputs (mocked API) without needing the real speech-to-text API. The core failure is code logic, not LLM or external API behavior. Need to wire together file handling, splitting, and merging modules with realistic test data.
behaviors.test_description: Create or use a realistic audio file >60 min (or synthetic test data). Mock speech-to-text API to return realistic segment outputs with speaker labels. Verify: (1) file is split at correct boundaries; (2) segments are processed in order; (3) merged summary preserves content and speaker continuity; (4) no duplicate or missing content; (5) speaker labels align across segments. Test with various segment boundaries and speaker patterns.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline (upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output) completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the multi-step pipeline fails, hangs, or requires manual intervention at any step. This involves two agentic steps (transcription via external API, summarization via LLM) in sequence, plus orchestration and error handling. Mocking either the transcription API or LLM would hide their real failure modes (timeouts, format changes, incomplete responses). The pipeline must work end-to-end with real dependencies to catch coordination failures, cascading errors, and non-deterministic behavior. This is explicitly a multi-agentic workflow.
behaviors.test_description: End-to-end test with real meeting recording (audio file). Call real (or staging) speech-to-text API, then real LLM summarization. Verify: (1) upload succeeds; (2) transcription completes and returns speaker-labeled output; (3) summary generation completes and returns structured output; (4) delivery/storage completes; (5) pipeline handles timeouts, retries, and partial failures gracefully; (6) no manual intervention required. Monitor latency and error rates.
